creator: kientzle@FreeBSD.org
text:
I definitely feel that ACL updates should follow chmod().
I believe that means that:
  * acl writes and updates touch ctime but no other timestamp
  * acl reads do not alter any timestamp

I think that EAs in the system namespace should also
behave like metadata, following the same time semantics
as above.

For EAs in the user namespace, it's more ambiguous
and I can see good arguments either way.  On the one
hand, there's an argument for treating all EAs the
same regardless of namespace, which would give the semantics
above for user EAs as well.  That would certainly
simplify documentation and implementation.

On the other hand, user EAs really are user data in
some sense, so I can see treating them as such, which
would give the following semantics:
  * User EA writes and updates touch mtime and ctime but not atime
  * User EA reads touch atime

I think both arguments are pretty equally strong.
I haven't played with this on other OSes yet; I wonder
how Linux or Solaris deal with this?

Tim


Greg Byshenk wrote:
> On Mon, Jan 26, 2009 at 06:43:13PM +0000, Robert Watson wrote:
> 
>>On Mon, 26 Jan 2009, Greg Byshenk wrote:
> 
> 
>>>>Well, this does raise an interesting question: should we also trim the 
>>>>setting of IN_ACCESS from ffs_extread() to prevent the access time from 
>>>>being updated on extended attribute read?  It sounds a bit like the 
>>>>answer is yes.
>>>
>>>Ok, for me it does not seem to solve the problem.
>>>
>>>7.1-STABLE sources as of 20 Jan 2009, patched as directed, complete build 
>>>of the system, but setting an ACL still changes file mtime.
> 
>  
> 
>>Indeed, and I can reproduce that here; I was testing with incremental ACL 
>>updates rather than the initial application which creates the EA.  Sounds 
>>like I've missed an IN_CHANGE somewhere, presumably somewhere where the 
>>block is actually allocated, so I'll go do a bit more code reading.  Any 
>>thoughts on the IN_ACCESS issue?
> 
> 
> Yes, ACL updates seem to work properly now.  Initial application does not.
> 
> As for the IN_ACCESS issue, I can see arguments either way.  Does changing
> permissions (non-ACLs) update atime?  My guess is that consistency/POLA
> suggests that setfacl should behave the same as chmod in this respect.
> 
>
